one_line_takeaway: A two-stage augmented-Lagrangian + Wasserstein-regularized gradient-projection unlearning method that explicitly protects the 'adjacent' retained data most entangled with the forget set, achieving near-zero forget-set accuracy without the retain-set collapse that afflicts strong-forgetting baselines.
key_finding: The method achieves near-complete forgetting while preserving the sensitive adjacent retain subset that baselines collapse. On CIFAR-100/ResNet-18 it reaches 0.00% train and 2.33% test accuracy on the forget set while keeping Dadj_r at 98.17% train / 78.17% test, whereas strong-forgetting baselines like DELETE and GDR crush Dadj_r to 3.57%/22.33% and 31.92%/22.33% respectively. The W2 regularization is shown to be the load-bearing component: removing it raises forget-set accuracy from 0.00%/2.33% back up to 18.87%/14.33% (train/test) while leaving retain performance largely intact, because plain PGD only preserves the MEAN forget loss and lets the loss distribution polarize so many samples stay correctly classified.
core_contribution: The paper's OWN contribution is a two-stage optimization framework for machine unlearning in the 'retain-forget entanglement' setting, where retained samples correlated with the forget set (an 'adjacent' retain subset) are disproportionately harmed by forgetting. Stage 1 uses an augmented Lagrangian method to aggressively raise the loss on the forget set Df while constraining the loss on the less-related 'remote' retain set Drem_r to equal its pre-unlearning value (adaptively updating a Lagrange multiplier rather than tuning a fixed penalty). Stage 2 introduces W-PGD: a first-order projected-gradient-descent step that updates on the adjacent retain set Dadj_r by projecting out gradient components aligned with the forget and remote losses, regularized by a Wasserstein-2 distance between the forget-set loss distributions before and after the update. The key conceptual move is decomposing the retain set into adjacent vs. remote subsets and reporting both separately, rather than averaging over the whole retain set (which the authors argue masks degradation on the sensitive correlated subset). Two propositions are proved: Prop 4.1 (the W-PGD step changes the forget and remote losses only to O(eta^2) while strictly decreasing the adjacent-retain loss to first order) and Prop 4.2 (an upper bound on post-update forget-set accuracy in terms of the W2 regularization weight alpha and the minimum forget-set loss).
threat_model: This is a privacy/safety-motivated unlearning paper, but it does NOT model an active adversary. The paper explicitly adopts the 'maximally reduce performance on the forget set' perspective (following Choi and Na, 2023) rather than the privacy-emulation / retrain-from-scratch indistinguishability perspective. The concrete risks it addresses are: (1) legal right-to-be-forgotten deletion requests and repairing models trained on poisoned/mislabeled or biased data; (2) the collateral risk that forgetting Df unintentionally degrades correlated retained data (e.g., forgetting toxic statements about a minority group harming non-toxic statements about the same group). Residual memorization is quantified via Membership Inference Attack (MIA) efficacy, but only in Appendix B (not in the main tables read here). There is no adversarial-robustness or attacker-capability model; the 'entanglement' is a distributional-overlap phenomenon, not an adversary.
limitations: (1) The adjacent/remote retain decomposition must be supplied a priori (same-superclass membership, or 'same demographic group') — the method assumes the entanglement structure is known, not discovered. (2) The paper adopts the 'destroy forget-set performance' objective, so it does not claim retrain-from-scratch indistinguishability; forgetting is not always driven to zero (ToxiGen forget-set accuracy is 11.95% train / 14.29% test; CelebA 25.48% test), so 'complete' unlearning is dataset-dependent. (3) Propositions rely on small step size eta and the smoothness/twice-differentiability of losses, and Prop 4.2's bound requires a large minimum forget-set loss (m > log n). (4) MIA efficacy, compute cost, and hyperparameter sensitivity are deferred to the appendix and not in the main results tables. (5) Retain preservation is not perfect: on CIFAR-100 test-set adjacent-retain accuracy drops from 80.00% (original) to 78.17%, and CelebA adjacent-retain test drops from 89.93% to 75.05%.

datasets_used: CIFAR-100 (20 superclasses x 5 subclasses; forget = 'aquarium fish' subclass in 'fish' superclass)
TinyImageNet (10 semantic superclasses; forget = 'dog' subclass in 'mammals' superclass)
ToxiGen (normal/toxic binary classification; forget = toxic sentences about LGBTQ groups mislabeled as normal under simulated biased pretraining)
CelebA (200K+ face images; 4-class Male x Smiling task; forget = female&not-smiling images that are not-Young and no-Eyeglasses)
quantitative_results: CIFAR-100/ResNet-18 (Table 1) Our method: train acc Df 0.00, Dadj_r 98.17, Drem_r 98.44; test acc Df 2.33, Dadj_r 78.17, Drem_r 81.10 (Original test: Df 90.00, Dadj_r 80.00, Drem_r 85.33)
CIFAR-100 baseline contrast: DELETE test Dadj_r 2.83, GDR test Dadj_r 22.33, SalUn test Dadj_r 34.90 - all collapse adjacent retain while forgetting; SSD test Drem_r only 67.23
ToxiGen/RoBERTa-base (Table 2) Our method: train Df 11.95, Dadj_r 88.88, Drem_r 92.73; test Df 14.29, Dadj_r 85.86, Drem_r 85.23 (best forget-set correction; GDR next best at 20.54 train Df)
CelebA/ViT-B (Table 3) Our method: test Df 25.48 (down from 81.37 original), Dadj_r 75.05, Drem_r 92.38; GA and DELETE reach Df 0.00 but collapse Dadj_r to 0.00
TinyImageNet/ViT (Table 4) Our method: train Df 0.00, Dadj_r 98.95, Drem_r 98.49; test Df 3.11, Dadj_r 91.27, Drem_r 88.88

W2 ablation (Table 5, CIFAR-100/ResNet-18): without W2 reg train Df 18.87 / test Df 14.33; with W2 reg train Df 0.00 / test Df 2.33 (retain performance similar), demonstrating W2 is necessary for forgetting
